Links: [{"title":"Troubleshoot organization policy errors for service accounts | Identity and Access Management (IAM) | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies"},{"title":"Fixing “Service Account Key Creation is Disabled” in Google Cloud - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/tahsin000/fixing-service-account-key-creation-is-disabled-in-google-cloud-3190"},{"title":"Service account key creation is disabled | Community","url":"https://security.googlecloudcommunity.com/google-security-operations-2/service-account-key-creation-is-disabled-213"},{"title":"Restrict IAM service account usage | Organization Policy | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/organization-policy/restrict-service-accounts"},{"title":"How to Enforce Service Account Key Creation Restrictions","url":"https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-17-how-to-enforce-service-account-key-creation-restrictions-with-organization-policies/view"},{"title":"Restricting service account usage | Resource Manager | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-service-accounts"},{"title":"Fixing Disabled Service Account Key Creation in a Google Cloud Project | Subsplash Support","url":"https://support.subsplash.com/en/articles/10829970-fixing-disabled-service-account-key-creation-in-a-google-cloud-project"},{"title":"Can't disable inherited IAM organization policy: Service account key creation blocked at project lev - Compute Infrastructure - Google Developer forums","url":"https://discuss.google.dev/t/cant-disable-inherited-iam-organization-policy-service-account-key-creation-blocked-at-project-lev/192976"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` organization policy and how to override it at the project level:

## Overview

The `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` legacy managed constraint disables the creation of new external service account keys and Cloud Storage HMAC keys, letting you control the use of unmanaged long-term credentials for service accounts. When this constraint is set, user-managed credentials cannot be created for service accounts in projects affected by the constraint.

## Project-Level Override

If you need to create a service account key, you can override the policy at the project level — if you have the right permissions.

### Steps to Override via Google Cloud Console

To override at the project level, select "Override parent's policy" and change the setting from "Enforced" to "Not enforced". This allows your project to create service account keys without changing the policy for the entire organization.

### Using gcloud Command

You can disable the org policy constraint `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` with a gcloud command. For example, to disable the policy for the project my-project, you would use the command: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies disable-enforce iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation --project=my-project`

## Important Considerations

- The owner role is insufficient for editing org policy. In the cloud console for the Organization (not the Project), you need to grant that user the "Organization Policy Administrator" role.
- You may also have to update the Legacy service account key creation constraint, as GCP often has a 'double-lock' system in place where if legacy or upload constraints are also active, just hitting one policy won't fully resolve the issue.

content.content:
# Google Cloud Security Baseline Constraints (May 3, 2024+)

## Enforced by Default Constraints

| Constraint ID | Description |
|---|---|
| `constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` | Blocks creation of persistent service account keys |
| `constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload` | Prevents uploading external public keys to service accounts |
| `constraints/iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts` | Stops default service accounts from receiving Editor role |
| `constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` | Restricts resource sharing to specified domain identities |
| `constraints/essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains` | Limits Essential Contacts to managed users in selected domains |
| `constraints/compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes` | Restricts protocol forwarding to internal IP addresses only |
| `constraints/storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess` | Prevents Cloud Storage buckets from using per-object ACLs |

## Key Details

**Cutoff Date:** "Google Cloud security baseline constraints are enforced for all organizations created on or after May 3, 2024."

**Impact on Existing Organizations:** Some organizations created between February and April 2024 may have these enforcements applied. Older organizations are not automatically affected.

**Notable Coverage:** The bundle addresses service account vulnerabilities, identity domain restrictions, storage access consistency, and compute networking exposure—but does not specifically mention Cloud Build constraints.

# Google Cloud Security Baseline Constraints (May 3, 2024+)

## Enforced by Default Constraints

| Constraint ID | Description |
|---|---|
| `constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` | Blocks creation of persistent service account keys |
| `constraints/iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload` | Prevents uploading external public keys to service accounts |
| `constraints/iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts` | Stops default service accounts from receiving Editor role |
| `constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` | Restricts resource sharing to specified domain identities |
| `constraints/essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains` | Limits Essential Contacts to managed users in selected domains |
| `constraints/compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes` | Restricts protocol forwarding to internal IP addresses only |
| `constraints/storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess` | Prevents Cloud Storage buckets from using per-object ACLs |

## Key Details

**Cutoff Date:** "Google Cloud security baseline constraints are enforced for all organizations created on or after May 3, 2024."

**Impact on Existing Organizations:** Some organizations created between February and April 2024 may have these enforcements applied. Older organizations are not automatically affected.

**Notable Coverage:** The bundle addresses service account vulnerabilities, identity domain restrictions, storage access consistency, and compute networking exposure—but does not specifically mention Cloud Build constraints.

content.text:
Here's the synthesis — and it reframes everything we hit today as **one root cause**, which is exactly the shape of your proposed heat.

## Root cause: scaleinvariant.org is a "secure-by-default" org

Google enforces a **security baseline bundle** automatically on **every org created on/after May 3, 2024** (some Feb–Apr 2024 orgs too). Older orgs — and *org-less* accounts like your old gmail payor — are **not** affected. So the entire "new territory" isn't incidental: moving the payor under a *recent* org switched on a bundle of constraints RBK was never designed against.

## The bundle, mapped against RBK

| Constraint | RBK impact |
|---|---|
| `iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` | **HIT today** — blocks governor/retriever/director key generation. The structural one. |
| `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` | **Likely hit** — restricts IAM-grant members to your domain; RBK's grants to SAs (and Google-managed agents) need auditing. |
| `iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload` | Related; RBK creates (not uploads) keys — probably clear, same family. |
| `iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts` | Check if RBK leans on default-SA Editor anywhere. |
| `storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess` | Levy bucket creation *succeeded*, so likely already compatible — confirm no per-object ACL use. |
| `essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains` | RBK doesn't touch essential contacts — low risk. |
| `compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes` | No GCP protocol forwarding in RBK — low risk. |

So the heat is really an **audit-and-adapt sweep**: one confirmed structural block (SA keys), one probable (member domains), and five to verify.

## Two strategic directions the heat must choose between

1. **Override path** — document/automate project-scoped org-policy exceptions for the constraints that bite. *Caveats:* needs **Organization Policy Administrator** (owner is insufficient), and these are **managed** constraints (`.managed.`), which override differently than legacy ones — a forum thread reports the project-level override of the inherited managed constraint being stubborn (a "double-lock" between managed and legacy variants).
2. **Keyless path** — move RBK credentials off downloadable SA keys to **workload identity federation / impersonation / short-lived tokens**. This makes RBK *natively* secure-by-default compatible (the direction Google is pushing), but it's a real redesign of the RBRA credential model.

The override path unblocks you this week; the keyless path is the durable answer. A "has-organization" heat probably scopes the override path first (to get productive) and lands keyless as a horizon item.

Sources:
- [Google Cloud security baseline constraints](https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/secure-by-default-organizations)
- [Introducing stronger default Org Policies (GCP blog)](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/introducing-stronger-default-org-policies-for-our-customers)
- [Troubleshoot org policy errors for service accounts](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies)
- [Restrict IAM service account usage](https://docs.cloud.google.com/organization-policy/restrict-service-accounts)

Want me to nominate this heat now? I'd suggest silks like **`rbk-has-organization-features`**, and — given it's an audit/redesign that needs planning before execution — created **stabled**, mirroring how we set up ₣BR. Tell me the silks and racing/stabled and I'll create it. (I can also live-probe the org for the *actually-enforced* set as the heat's first scoping input, since enforcement can be tuned per-org.)

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BS

## Shape

This heat exists because RBK's credential and resource model was designed against an **org-less** GCP account (the original gmail payor) and breaks under a **secure-by-default organization**. Google enforces a security-baseline org-policy bundle automatically on every organization created on/after 2024-05-03; scaleinvariant.org is such an org, while org-less accounts and older orgs never see it. Moving the payor under the org is what activated the friction — a structural mismatch, not incidental breakage. The depot *infrastructure* (project, pools, builds, GAR, bucket) levies cleanly under the org; the conflict is confined to the operational-role credential layer.

## Constraint bundle vs RBK

The secure-by-default managed constraints and their RBK bearing:

- `iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` — confirmed blocker. RBRA credentials are downloadable SA keys; governor mantle and retriever/director invest each generate one, refused HTTP 400 "Key creation is not allowed."
- `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` — probable. Restricts IAM-grant members to org-domain identities; RBK grants to SAs and Google-managed agents need auditing.
- `iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload` — related family; RBK creates rather than uploads, likely clear.
- `iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts` — verify RBK never relies on default-SA Editor.
- `storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess` — depot bucket already created under it; confirm no per-object ACL use.
- `essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains`, `compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes` — not exercised by RBK; low risk.

## The strategic fork (undecided — why this heat is stabled)

1. Override path — project-scoped org-policy exceptions for the biting constraints. Unblocks fast. Costs: needs Organization Policy Administrator (owner insufficient); managed constraints' project-level override of an inherited org policy is reportedly stubborn (managed-vs-legacy double-lock). Leaves RBK dependent on long-lived SA keys, against the platform's direction.

2. Identity / keyless path — re-express retriever and director as Google identities (OAuth user / workload identity federation / impersonation) rather than SA-key-bearing service accounts: short-lived credentials, no downloadable keys, natively secure-by-default compatible. A larger change touching the RBRA credential model end to end. An existing exploration in the project's JJ records already gestures at binding retriever and director directly to Google email identities; that idea is the seed of this path.

## What done looks like

A decision on override vs identity-keyless (plausibly override-to-unblock now, keyless as the durable target); an audit verdict on each bundle constraint against RBK flows; and, for the chosen path, the concrete changes to credential install, regime files, and the RBS* depot / SA-invest specs. Until that decision lands, this heat holds shape only.

Did both scouts (the `oauth` scout, plus a discriminating `director oauth identity` scout — bare `director` would just flood like `retriever` did). Honest result: **no slated pace or heat explicitly explores retriever/director as full Google OAuth identities.**

What the `oauth` scout actually surfaced (filtering out the paddock-boilerplate matches, where whole heats match because "OAuth" appears in their paddock):

- **₢AqAAC** (oauth-token-lifetime-extension, ₣Aq) — token *lifetime*, not identity binding.
- **₢BBAAp** (oauth-token-mint-retry-on-signature-race, ₣BB) — token *mint mechanics*.
- **₢AUAA5** (foundry-and-cloud-step-trust-audit, ₣AU) — references a prior `oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep`, a trust review.

All three orbit the **payor** OAuth model (which already exists) or token plumbing — none proposes converting the **operational roles** (retriever/director) to OAuth identities. The `director oauth identity` scout returned **zero**.

So the idea you're recalling isn't a pace the scout indexes. Most likely it lives as one of:
- an **itch** in `jji_itch.md` (scout may not cover the itch ledger unless flagged actionable),
- a **memo** under `Memos/`, or
- a thread that stayed conversational and never got slated.

Want me to (a) re-scout with `actionable: true` to sweep the itch ledger, or (b) grep `Memos/` for the retriever/director-as-identity concept? Either could find it; if both come up empty, it was never written down — in which case the ₣BS paddock's keyless-path description is now the first durable capture of it, and you may just want to drop the "existing exploration" reference I put there.

content.content:
1	# Google-Native Human Authentication — Design Exploration
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-27
4	
5	## Problem
6	
7	Today's Recipe Bottle credential model distributes service-account JSON
8	key files (the RBRA pattern) for Governor, Director, and Retriever
9	roles.  For a single-developer or small-team project this works fine.
10	For wider adoption, two frictions emerge:
11	
12	1. **Distribution logistics.**  Whoever holds Payor (or Governor) must
13	   generate an SA key, ship it via secure channel to each user, and
14	   the user must place it on disk at the regime-defined path.
15	   Multiply across multiple Depots, multiple roles, multiple users.
16	
17	2. **Revocation hygiene.**  SA keys do not expire.  Revoking access
18	   means rotating the key (and re-issuing to legitimate users), then
19	   trusting that old copies are gone.  There is no central "kill this
20	   person's access" button.
21	
22	The natural ask from a future external user: "I have a Google account,
23	why can't I just log into Chrome and use this?"
24	
25	## Approaches Evaluated
26	
27	### A. Status quo (RBRA keyfile distribution)
28	
29	Current state.  Payor generates SA key → ships to user → user places at
30	regime-defined path → all subsequent operations use the key file
31	directly via the existing JWT-bearer flow in RBGO.
32	
33	- **Pros:** works with no cloud-side identity management; user needs
34	  nothing Google-specific installed.
35	- **Cons:** all the distribution and revocation problems above.
36	
37	### B. gcloud SDK + Application Default Credentials + impersonation
38	
39	User installs gcloud SDK, runs `gcloud auth login`, gets
40	`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the target SA, uses
41	`--impersonate-service-account` (or sets
42	`auth/impersonate_service_account` config) to act as the SA.
43	
44	- **Pros:** Google's recommended path.  Well-documented.  Token
45	  caching and credential discovery are handled by the SDK.
46	- **Cons:** requires gcloud SDK install — large dependency surface
47	  relative to Recipe Bottle's current minimal stack (curl, openssl,
48	  jq).  Project policy has consistently rejected gcloud as a
49	  prerequisite for the same surface-area reasons.
50	
51	Verdict: dismissed.
52	
53	### C. REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation (recommended)
54	
55	Replicates the existing Payor OAuth pattern (RBGP/RBGO) for a new
56	"user impersonation" OAuth client.  User authenticates in a browser
57	once; refresh token cached locally.  When an operation needs to act as
58	Governor/Director/Retriever, mint a user access token from the
59	refresh token, then call
60	
61	```
62	POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{sa-email}:generateAccessToken
63	Authorization: Bearer <user access token>
64	```
65	
66	to obtain a short-lived SA access token.  Use that SA token in the
67	`Authorization: Bearer` header for the actual API call.
68	
69	- **Pros:**
70	  - No new dependency: reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack.
71	  - SA principal preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role
72	    taxonomy, audit identity all unchanged.
73	  - Distribution dissolves: nothing to ship; access is granted by IAM
74	    binding (server-side state).
75	  - Revocation: one IAM binding deletion, or the user can revoke
76	    OAuth consent at myaccount.google.com.  No artifact to chase.
77	  - Audit logs gain `delegationInfo`: "user X impersonated SA Y at
78	    time Z" — better forensics than today's invisible-human keyfile
79	    model.
80	- **Cons:**
81	  - Project must register a new OAuth client (app identity for the
82	    impersonation flow); analogous to today's Payor OAuth client.
83	  - Two extra REST calls per operation (token refresh + impersonation
84	    exchange), both cacheable for the token's lifetime.
85	  - Coexistence with RBRA: probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) and any
86	    credential-mode-aware machinery must handle both modes.
87	
88	## Mechanism Detail
89	
90	### Authentication flow
91	
92	1. **Once per user (sign-in).**  User runs a tabtarget — provisional
93	   shape: an OnboardingCredentialBrowser entry — which opens a
94	   localhost callback listener and launches the system browser to
95	   Google's OAuth consent endpoint.  User signs in with their Google
96	   account, consents to the `cloud-platform` scope, the authorization
97	   code returns to localhost, and the tabtarget exchanges it for a
98	   refresh token via `oauth2.googleapis.com/token`.  Refresh token
99	   cached locally.
100	2. **Once per user (admin-side grant).**  Governor (or whoever
101	   administers the Depot's SA roster) grants the user
102	   `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on each SA the user is
103	   authorized to act as.  This is a new Governor verb operating on
104	   SA IAM policy — sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
105	3. **Per operation.**  Tabtarget refreshes the user access token (POST
106	   to `oauth2.googleapis.com/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token`)
107	   if the cached one has expired, then calls `generateAccessToken` on
108	   the impersonation endpoint to obtain an SA access token (TTL ≤ 1
109	   hour, configurable).  Use SA token in `Authorization: Bearer` for
110	   the API call.
111	
112	### Storage shape
113	
114	Two persistent items, parallel to today's Payor OAuth:
115	
116	| Artifact | Origin | Sensitivity |
117	|---|---|---|
118	| OAuth client ID + secret | Project owner registers once at console.cloud.google.com, ships in repo or installed via existing JSON-ingest pattern (analogous to RBSPI) | App identity, not user-secret |
119	| User refresh token | Generated by browser consent, lands locally after first sign-in | Long-lived, sensitive |
120	
121	Two transient caches (re-mintable from the refresh token):
122	
123	- User access token (~1 hour)
124	- SA access token from `generateAccessToken` (≤ 1 hour, configurable)
125	
126	Storage location: open question (see below).  In all cases: 0600
127	permissions, gitignored.
128	
129	### IAM binding model
130	
131	Service accounts in GCP are dual-natured: identities (principals that
132	act) and resources (with their own IAM policies).  The grant lives in
133	IAM policy on the SA itself:
134	
135	```
136	Resource: governor@<depot>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
137	  Binding: roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator
138	  Member:  user:alice@example.com
139	```
140	
141	Multi-Depot, multi-role, multi-user is a sparse three-axis matrix
142	populated by IAM bindings on SAs across Depots:
143	
144	```
145	                  Depot D1              Depot D2
146	alice@...     Governor, Director    Director
147	bob@...       Director              Retriever
148	carol@...     —                     Governor, Director, Retriever
149	```
150	
151	Grants do not span Depots — each Depot is its own boundary.  Discovery
152	from the user side: enumerate SAs in a Depot, check each SA's IAM
153	policy for the user's email — no central registry needed because the
154	policies on the SAs are the registry.
155	
156	### Administrative home
157	
158	Naturally extends Governor.  Today Governor mantles itself, charters
159	Retriever, knights Director, forfeits SAs — managing the SA roster
160	within its Depot.  Adding a verb for "grant user X impersonation
161	rights on SA Y" sits in the same module operating on the same kind
162	of object (SA IAM policy).  A complementary verb removes the binding.
163	
164	## Coexistence with RBRA
165	
166	Additive in the near term, not a replacement:
167	
168	- Headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style
169	  scenarios) still need keyfiles or workload identity.
170	- Existing users with working RBRA setups do not need to migrate.
171	- A regime mode field selects credential mode per role (provisional:
172	  `RBRA_MODE=keyfile|impersonation`); the token-mint helper branches
173	  on mode at the lowest layer, leaving downstream callers unchanged.
174	- Credential probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) gain mode dispatch.
175	
176	Eventual deprecation of the keyfile mode for human-driven operations
177	is a separate decision, triggered by adoption signal — not part of
178	this work.
179	
180	## Threat Model Summary
181	
182	| Concern | RBRA keyfile (today) | OAuth + impersonation |
183	|---|---|---|
184	| Artifact on disk | SA private key (long-lived) | User refresh token (revocable) |
185	| Workstation compromise | SA fully compromised until rotation | User can revoke OAuth consent; admin can drop IAM binding |
186	| Revocation | Hunt all key copies, rotate | Single IAM binding deletion |
187	| Audit attribution | "SA did X" — human invisible | "User X impersonated SA at time Z" |
188	| Required user state | JSON file at known path | Refresh token at known path + IAM binding admin-side |
189	
190	The proposed mechanism is strictly stronger on every axis the existing
191	RBRA model addresses, with the caveat that it requires a one-time
192	admin grant per (user, SA) pair rather than a one-time keyfile handoff.
193	
194	## Open Questions
195	
196	- **Storage location.**  Regime-tree (per-checkout, gitignored, dies
197	  with `git clean -fdx`) or `$HOME`-rooted (per-user, multiplexes
198	  across checkouts, survives clean operations)?  The Payor refresh
199	  token's current home is the obvious starting point.
200	- **OAuth client scope.**  One project-wide OAuth client (registered
201	  once at the project level, used by all installations) or per-Manor
202	  (each Manor registers its own)?  Tradeoff: shared client simplifies
203	  onboarding but ties all users to the project's OAuth consent
204	  screen and ToS.
205	- **Onboarding handbook track.**  Parallel RBHO* track
206	  (OnboardingCredentialBrowser) alongside the existing keyfile
207	  tracks, or a unified track that branches on user choice?
208	- **Probe coexistence.**  Should existing probes auto-detect mode
209	  from regime, or should there be separate probes per mode?
210	- **Mode field naming and placement.**  Does the mode live on the
211	  RBRA file itself, on the regime that points to it, or as a
212	  separate per-role regime field?  Affects how the probe stack
213	  loads.
214	
215	## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items
216	
217	No direct dependencies.  Indirectly relates to "Credential confinement
218	to crucible" (Crucible Conduit Architecture) in spirit — both move
219	credentials toward shorter-lived, more bounded forms — but the
220	mechanisms are independent.  Credential confinement is about where
221	credentials live within the workstation/crucible split; this work is
222	about whether the workstation-side credentials are static keys or
223	browser-minted tokens.
224	
225	## Trigger to Revisit
226	
227	Either:
228	
229	- First concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding.
230	- First time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption (rather
231	  than being theoretically frictionful).
232	
233	## Notes on Conversation Origin
234	
235	Captured from a design conversation on 2026-04-27 between the project
236	owner and Claude.  The conversation traced the constraint chain:
237	"users won't want to distribute RBRA files" → impersonation pattern →
238	"does this require gcloud?" → REST-only flow against
239	`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` → "where does the security state
240	live?" → storage shape → "how does the user tie to roles?" → IAM
241	bindings on SAs as resources.  The key turn was recognizing that the
242	Payor OAuth machinery already proves the harder primitive (browser
243	auth without gcloud); adding human-to-SA impersonation is a small
244	extension on top, not a new dependency layer.
245

# Google-Native Human Authentication — Design Exploration

Date: 2026-04-27

## Problem

Today's Recipe Bottle credential model distributes service-account JSON
key files (the RBRA pattern) for Governor, Director, and Retriever
roles.  For a single-developer or small-team project this works fine.
For wider adoption, two frictions emerge:

1. **Distribution logistics.**  Whoever holds Payor (or Governor) must
   generate an SA key, ship it via secure channel to each user, and
   the user must place it on disk at the regime-defined path.
   Multiply across multiple Depots, multiple roles, multiple users.

2. **Revocation hygiene.**  SA keys do not expire.  Revoking access
   means rotating the key (and re-issuing to legitimate users), then
   trusting that old copies are gone.  There is no central "kill this
   person's access" button.

The natural ask from a future external user: "I have a Google account,
why can't I just log into Chrome and use this?"

## Approaches Evaluated

### A. Status quo (RBRA keyfile distribution)

Current state.  Payor generates SA key → ships to user → user places at
regime-defined path → all subsequent operations use the key file
directly via the existing JWT-bearer flow in RBGO.

- **Pros:** works with no cloud-side identity management; user needs
  nothing Google-specific installed.
- **Cons:** all the distribution and revocation problems above.

### B. gcloud SDK + Application Default Credentials + impersonation

User installs gcloud SDK, runs `gcloud auth login`, gets
`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on the target SA, uses
`--impersonate-service-account` (or sets
`auth/impersonate_service_account` config) to act as the SA.

- **Pros:** Google's recommended path.  Well-documented.  Token
  caching and credential discovery are handled by the SDK.
- **Cons:** requires gcloud SDK install — large dependency surface
  relative to Recipe Bottle's current minimal stack (curl, openssl,
  jq).  Project policy has consistently rejected gcloud as a
  prerequisite for the same surface-area reasons.

Verdict: dismissed.

### C. REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation (recommended)

Replicates the existing Payor OAuth pattern (RBGP/RBGO) for a new
"user impersonation" OAuth client.  User authenticates in a browser
once; refresh token cached locally.  When an operation needs to act as
Governor/Director/Retriever, mint a user access token from the
refresh token, then call

```
POST https://iamcredentials.googleapis.com/v1/projects/-/serviceAccounts/{sa-email}:generateAccessToken
Authorization: Bearer <user access token>
```

to obtain a short-lived SA access token.  Use that SA token in the
`Authorization: Bearer` header for the actual API call.

- **Pros:**
  - No new dependency: reuses the existing curl/openssl/jq stack.
  - SA principal preserved — RBSCIG contracts, RBGI machinery, role
    taxonomy, audit identity all unchanged.
  - Distribution dissolves: nothing to ship; access is granted by IAM
    binding (server-side state).
  - Revocation: one IAM binding deletion, or the user can revoke
    OAuth consent at myaccount.google.com.  No artifact to chase.
  - Audit logs gain `delegationInfo`: "user X impersonated SA Y at
    time Z" — better forensics than today's invisible-human keyfile
    model.
- **Cons:**
  - Project must register a new OAuth client (app identity for the
    impersonation flow); analogous to today's Payor OAuth client.
  - Two extra REST calls per operation (token refresh + impersonation
    exchange), both cacheable for the token's lifetime.
  - Coexistence with RBRA: probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) and any
    credential-mode-aware machinery must handle both modes.

## Mechanism Detail

### Authentication flow

1. **Once per user (sign-in).**  User runs a tabtarget — provisional
   shape: an OnboardingCredentialBrowser entry — which opens a
   localhost callback listener and launches the system browser to
   Google's OAuth consent endpoint.  User signs in with their Google
   account, consents to the `cloud-platform` scope, the authorization
   code returns to localhost, and the tabtarget exchanges it for a
   refresh token via `oauth2.googleapis.com/token`.  Refresh token
   cached locally.
2. **Once per user (admin-side grant).**  Governor (or whoever
   administers the Depot's SA roster) grants the user
   `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` on each SA the user is
   authorized to act as.  This is a new Governor verb operating on
   SA IAM policy — sibling to mantle/charter/knight/forfeit.
3. **Per operation.**  Tabtarget refreshes the user access token (POST
   to `oauth2.googleapis.com/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token`)
   if the cached one has expired, then calls `generateAccessToken` on
   the impersonation endpoint to obtain an SA access token (TTL ≤ 1
   hour, configurable).  Use SA token in `Authorization: Bearer` for
   the API call.

### Storage shape

Two persistent items, parallel to today's Payor OAuth:

| Artifact | Origin | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| OAuth client ID + secret | Project owner registers once at console.cloud.google.com, ships in repo or installed via existing JSON-ingest pattern (analogous to RBSPI) | App identity, not user-secret |
| User refresh token | Generated by browser consent, lands locally after first sign-in | Long-lived, sensitive |

Two transient caches (re-mintable from the refresh token):

- User access token (~1 hour)
- SA access token from `generateAccessToken` (≤ 1 hour, configurable)

Storage location: open question (see below).  In all cases: 0600
permissions, gitignored.

### IAM binding model

Service accounts in GCP are dual-natured: identities (principals that
act) and resources (with their own IAM policies).  The grant lives in
IAM policy on the SA itself:

```
Resource: governor@<depot>.iam.gserviceaccount.com
  Binding: roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator
  Member:  user:alice@example.com
```

Multi-Depot, multi-role, multi-user is a sparse three-axis matrix
populated by IAM bindings on SAs across Depots:

```
                  Depot D1              Depot D2
alice@...     Governor, Director    Director
bob@...       Director              Retriever
carol@...     —                     Governor, Director, Retriever
```

Grants do not span Depots — each Depot is its own boundary.  Discovery
from the user side: enumerate SAs in a Depot, check each SA's IAM
policy for the user's email — no central registry needed because the
policies on the SAs are the registry.

### Administrative home

Naturally extends Governor.  Today Governor mantles itself, charters
Retriever, knights Director, forfeits SAs — managing the SA roster
within its Depot.  Adding a verb for "grant user X impersonation
rights on SA Y" sits in the same module operating on the same kind
of object (SA IAM policy).  A complementary verb removes the binding.

## Coexistence with RBRA

Additive in the near term, not a replacement:

- Headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style
  scenarios) still need keyfiles or workload identity.
- Existing users with working RBRA setups do not need to migrate.
- A regime mode field selects credential mode per role (provisional:
  `RBRA_MODE=keyfile|impersonation`); the token-mint helper branches
  on mode at the lowest layer, leaving downstream callers unchanged.
- Credential probes (RBSAJ, RBSAO, RBSAV) gain mode dispatch.

Eventual deprecation of the keyfile mode for human-driven operations
is a separate decision, triggered by adoption signal — not part of
this work.

## Threat Model Summary

| Concern | RBRA keyfile (today) | OAuth + impersonation |
|---|---|---|
| Artifact on disk | SA private key (long-lived) | User refresh token (revocable) |
| Workstation compromise | SA fully compromised until rotation | User can revoke OAuth consent; admin can drop IAM binding |
| Revocation | Hunt all key copies, rotate | Single IAM binding deletion |
| Audit attribution | "SA did X" — human invisible | "User X impersonated SA at time Z" |
| Required user state | JSON file at known path | Refresh token at known path + IAM binding admin-side |

The proposed mechanism is strictly stronger on every axis the existing
RBRA model addresses, with the caveat that it requires a one-time
admin grant per (user, SA) pair rather than a one-time keyfile handoff.

## Open Questions

- **Storage location.**  Regime-tree (per-checkout, gitignored, dies
  with `git clean -fdx`) or `$HOME`-rooted (per-user, multiplexes
  across checkouts, survives clean operations)?  The Payor refresh
  token's current home is the obvious starting point.
- **OAuth client scope.**  One project-wide OAuth client (registered
  once at the project level, used by all installations) or per-Manor
  (each Manor registers its own)?  Tradeoff: shared client simplifies
  onboarding but ties all users to the project's OAuth consent
  screen and ToS.
- **Onboarding handbook track.**  Parallel RBHO* track
  (OnboardingCredentialBrowser) alongside the existing keyfile
  tracks, or a unified track that branches on user choice?
- **Probe coexistence.**  Should existing probes auto-detect mode
  from regime, or should there be separate probes per mode?
- **Mode field naming and placement.**  Does the mode live on the
  RBRA file itself, on the regime that points to it, or as a
  separate per-role regime field?  Affects how the probe stack
  loads.

## Relationship to Existing Roadmap Items

No direct dependencies.  Indirectly relates to "Credential confinement
to crucible" (Crucible Conduit Architecture) in spirit — both move
credentials toward shorter-lived, more bounded forms — but the
mechanisms are independent.  Credential confinement is about where
credentials live within the workstation/crucible split; this work is
about whether the workstation-side credentials are static keys or
browser-minted tokens.

## Trigger to Revisit

Either:

- First concrete external-user request for browser-based onboarding.
- First time keyfile distribution materially blocks adoption (rather
  than being theoretically frictionful).

## Notes on Conversation Origin

Captured from a design conversation on 2026-04-27 between the project
owner and Claude.  The conversation traced the constraint chain:
"users won't want to distribute RBRA files" → impersonation pattern →
"does this require gcloud?" → REST-only flow against
`iamcredentials.googleapis.com` → "where does the security state
live?" → storage shape → "how does the user tie to roles?" → IAM
bindings on SAs as resources.  The key turn was recognizing that the
Payor OAuth machinery already proves the harder primitive (browser
auth without gcloud); adding human-to-SA impersonation is a small
extension on top, not a new dependency layer.

content.text:
Found it — and it's far more than a passing idea. **`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`** (2026-04-27) is a complete design exploration of exactly this.

## What it proposes

Three approaches evaluated; **Approach C is recommended**: **REST-only browser OAuth + impersonation.**
- The human authenticates once via browser OAuth (explicitly *reusing the existing payor OAuth machinery* — no new dependency), refresh token cached locally.
- Per operation, mint a user token, then call `iamcredentials.googleapis.com/.../{sa-email}:generateAccessToken` to get a short-lived SA token.
- The human↔role binding *is* a Google email identity on the SA's IAM policy: `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`, member `user:alice@example.com`. The SA principal is preserved; distribution dissolves; revocation is one IAM-binding deletion.
- Notably, it **rejects** the gcloud-SDK path (Approach B) for the same minimal-stack reasons RBK already enforces.

## Why this is pivotal for ₣BS

This isn't just "the keyless direction" — **it's the direct dissolution of today's org-policy block.** `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` blocks key *creation*; Approach C **creates no keys at all**. The durable answer to the secure-by-default org wall was already designed a month ago, before we hit the wall.

**But one load-bearing gap ₣BS must own:** the memo explicitly carves out *headless* paths — "Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit-style scenarios still need keyfiles or workload identity." Those are exactly the gauntlet/fixture flows. Under a secure-by-default org, browser-OAuth-impersonation fixes **human-driven** ops but leaves **automated** ops still needing keys — which the org forbids → they'd require **workload identity federation**, which the memo doesn't cover. So ₣BS = (memo's Approach C for humans) + (a WIF story for headless), not the memo alone.

This is now a concrete, durable artifact to anchor the paddock on. Want me to update the ₣BS paddock — replace the vague "an existing exploration gestures at this" with a direct pointer to `memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md`, and fold in the two framings this surfaced (keyless = the org-policy unblock; headless paths still need WIF)? Memo path is a durable reference, so it's paddock-discipline-clean.

message.content: yes, update the paddock with the memo reference.  I do want this chat to reach a more firm resolution though, and when I stepped away today I lost track of our issues in this chat.

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BS

## Shape

This heat exists because RBK's credential and resource model was designed against an **org-less** GCP account (the original gmail payor) and breaks under a **secure-by-default organization**. Google enforces a security-baseline org-policy bundle automatically on every organization created on/after 2024-05-03; scaleinvariant.org is such an org, while org-less accounts and older orgs never see it. Moving the payor under the org is what activated the friction — a structural mismatch, not incidental breakage. The depot *infrastructure* (project, pools, builds, GAR, bucket) levies cleanly under the org; the conflict is confined to the operational-role credential layer.

## Constraint bundle vs RBK

The secure-by-default managed constraints and their RBK bearing:

- `iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` — confirmed blocker. RBRA credentials are downloadable SA keys; governor mantle and retriever/director invest each generate one, refused HTTP 400 "Key creation is not allowed."
- `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` — probable. Restricts IAM-grant members to org-domain identities; RBK grants to SAs and Google-managed agents need auditing.
- `iam.managed.disableServiceAccountKeyUpload` — related family; RBK creates rather than uploads, likely clear.
- `iam.automaticIamGrantsForDefaultServiceAccounts` — verify RBK never relies on default-SA Editor.
- `storage.uniformBucketLevelAccess` — depot bucket already created under it; confirm no per-object ACL use.
- `essentialcontacts.managed.allowedContactDomains`, `compute.managed.restrictProtocolForwardingCreationForTypes` — not exercised by RBK; low risk.

## The strategic fork (undecided — why this heat is stabled)

1. Override path — project-scoped org-policy exceptions for the biting constraints. Unblocks fast. Costs: needs Organization Policy Administrator (owner insufficient); managed constraints' project-level override of an inherited org policy is reportedly stubborn (managed-vs-legacy double-lock). Leaves RBK dependent on long-lived SA keys, against the platform's direction.

2. Identity / keyless path — re-express retriever and director as Google identities so no downloadable SA key is ever created. This *dissolves* the confirmed blocker rather than overriding it: the constraint forbids key creation, and this path creates none. A full design already exists in `Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (Approach C, recommended): browser OAuth reusing the existing payor machinery, then `iamcredentials …:generateAccessToken` impersonation; the human-to-role binding is a Google email on the SA's IAM policy (`roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`, member `user:...`). The SA principal, role taxonomy, and audit identity are preserved; distribution dissolves; revocation is one IAM-binding deletion. The memo also rejects the gcloud-SDK path (Approach B) for RBK's minimal-stack (curl/openssl/jq) reasons.

   Gap the memo does not close: it covers human-driven operations only and explicitly leaves headless paths (Cloud Build itself, automated fixtures, Ifrit scenarios, the gauntlet) on keyfiles-or-workload-identity. Under a secure-by-default org those automated paths stay blocked, so this heat must add a **workload-identity-federation** story for them. So ₣BS scope = memo Approach C (human-driven roles) + a WIF answer (headless/automated), not the memo alone.

## What done looks like

A decision on override vs identity-keyless (plausibly override-to-unblock now, keyless as the durable target); an audit verdict on each bundle constraint against RBK flows; a workload-identity-federation answer for headless paths; and, for the chosen path, the concrete changes to credential install, regime files, and the RBS* depot / SA-invest specs. Until that decision lands, this heat holds shape only.

message.content: Lets discuss the import of (a): we have never set up a depot with a payor project with an organization.  Is it easy to add this permission either in our depot levy or the payor establish procedures?  Will it break 'no orginization' model?

content.text:
Good — this is the right thing to pin down before touching (a), because "where the override lives" and "does it fork the model" are the real costs, not the override itself.

## Easy in the handbook; risky in the levy

**Payor establish (handbook) — easy and clean.** The establish procedure *already* forks at "No organization (this guide's path) vs organization affiliation (advanced)" — line 79 of `rbhpe_establish.sh`. Adding a **conditional manual step** under the advanced branch ("if you affiliated with an org, also grant yourself Organization Policy Administrator and set an override for `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`") is purely additive. The no-org operator never sees it. Zero code-path risk.

**Depot levy (code) — harder, and I'd resist it.** To automate the override in `rbgp_depot_levy` you'd need:
- The **payor OAuth identity to hold `orgpolicy.policyAdmin`** — an org-level role, far heavier than the project-owner it has today. That's a real privilege escalation baked into the payor.
- A **guard** so it only fires when the depot is org-parented (else the `setOrgPolicy` call errors — there's no policy to set on an org-less project).
- The philosophical wart: the levy would be **auto-disabling a security control** on every depot it creates. The automation that builds your depot also punches a hole in your org's baseline.
- It **doesn't generalize**: an external adopter under their own org may not be org-policy-admin, so the levy can't assume it.

## Does it break the "no organization" model?

**Functionally, no — if guarded.** The org-less path has *no org policies at all*; `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` simply isn't in effect there, so it needs no override and an org-membership guard leaves it untouched.

**But it forks the model**, and that's the actual cost. You'd go from one model to two: *no-org* (simple, key creation just works) and *has-org* (carries the override burden + the heavier payor permission). Two modes to document, test, and maintain — permanently. Not breakage, but a standing bifurcation.

## Two tensions worth sitting with

1. **It's self-defeating.** You *opted into* a secure-by-default org, then (a) drills through its baseline on the depot to keep RBK's key model alive — producing a third state: "has-org-but-with-a-security-hole," arguably worse than either clean endpoint. The keyless path (₣BS / the 2026-04-27 memo) is the *coherent* has-org answer; (a) is a stopgap that fights the very posture you chose.
2. **"Easy" may be optimistic.** These are **managed** constraints, and the research flagged that project-level override of an inherited managed constraint can be stubborn (managed-vs-legacy double-lock) — it may force you to the **org level**, which broadens the blast radius from one depot to everything. Until that's verified, I wouldn't promise project-scoped works.

## My read

Keep the codebase out of it for now. **You're the org admin of scaleinvariant.org**, so do the override **manually, once, by hand** to unblock `…100001` today — don't bake it into levy or establish yet. That gets you productive without committing RBK to a forked model, and leaves the real architecture choice (override-as-policy vs keyless) to ₣BS where it belongs.

Want me to verify the override actually takes at project scope (read-only check of whether the managed constraint accepts a project-level override) before you decide — or is this enough to settle the discussion?

message.content: The philosophy by which we choose what goes in 'payor establish' versus 'levy' is to drive everything automatable into the levy.  Is this a 'per depot' attribute?

content.text:
Right — I had the philosophy backwards. Automatable → levy; establish holds only the irreducibly-manual console steps. So the real question is exactly the one you're asking: **is the override a per-depot attribute?** Let me split it, because the answer is "partly," and the split decides where it lives.

## The override is two things, with different cardinality

1. **The capability** — the payor identity holding `orgpolicy.policyAdmin`. This is **not** per-depot. It's a one-time grant, it's org-scoped, and the payor **can't grant it to itself** — an org admin does it once. That's irreducibly establish/setup territory (arguably even pre-RBK).
2. **The action** — actually setting `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` to *not enforced*. This is the part whose cardinality is in question.

## Whether the *action* is per-depot is a design choice — gated by one fact

- **Project-scoped override** → it lives on each depot project. Since the levy creates each depot project (and we confirmed they auto-parent under the org), this **is** a genuine per-depot action → by your philosophy, it belongs **in the levy**, with the establish ceremony having set up the `policyAdmin` precondition once. Tight blast radius: only RBK depot projects get the hole; the rest of the org keeps the baseline.
- **Org/folder-scoped override** → set once, all depots inherit it. **Not** per-depot at all → establish/setup, broader hole.

So "is it per-depot?" resolves to: **it's per-depot if and only if the managed constraint accepts a project-scoped override.** And that's the empirical unknown the research flagged — managed constraints (the `iam.managed.*` / legacy double-lock) sometimes *refuse* project-level override and force you to org level. If they refuse, the action collapses to org-scope → once → **not** a levy concern. If they accept, it's cleanly a per-depot levy step.

That's checkable read-only right now (attempt to read/dry-run a project-scoped policy on `…100001`), and it's the fact that settles where this belongs.

## Two caveats before you let it into the levy

- **Granting the payor `orgpolicy.policyAdmin` is heavy** — that identity could then rewrite *any* org policy, not just this one. Putting that power behind the OAuth payor to enable a per-levy override is a real escalation of what a payor *is*.
- **It still automates security-control-disabling into your build path.** The keyless route sidesteps the entire per-depot/levy/establish question — no key created means no override needed *anywhere*, at any cardinality.

Want me to run the read-only feasibility check on project-scoped override of the managed constraint? That single fact tells us whether this is even *eligible* to be the per-depot levy step you're describing, or whether it's forced to be a one-time org-level setup.

message.content: Please try and restate in simpler language, I'm a bit overwhelmed right now.  Lets try to do less per exchange for now.

content.text:
Of course. Here's the simple version:

**Your question — is the override a per-depot thing?**

It splits in two:

1. **Permission to do it** — granted once to your payor, by an org admin. Not per-depot. (Belongs in establish.)
2. **The actual override** — *might* be per-depot, or might have to be set once for the whole org. We don't yet know which Google allows.

So: **we can't say if it's per-depot until we test one thing** — whether Google lets you set this override on a single project, or forces it org-wide.

Want me to run that one test? It's read-only and it answers the per-depot question directly.

content.text:
Clean result. Side by side:

That's the whole difference in one picture. Want me to stop here so it can settle, or take the next small step?

content: Goal: get a usable depot under your new org. The depot is built but its service-account credentials are blocked by an organization security policy. Next: decide whether to manually turn that policy off for the depot, or go keyless. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: Help me understand the ramifications of an override.  IS that configuring the organization itself?  Is that a 'depot level' attribute that just exists for the depot project?

content.text:
Good question, and it's the heart of it. Simple version:

Think of the org rule as flowing **downhill** — from the organization down onto every project beneath it.

An override is a sign that says "this rule doesn't apply here." You can post that sign in one of two places:

1. **On the organization** → the rule changes for **everything** under it (every project, now and future). That *is* reconfiguring the organization.
2. **On just the depot project** → the rule still holds everywhere else; only that one depot is exempt. That's a **depot-level** attribute — it lives on the depot project and nowhere else.

So: an override **does not have to** touch the organization. It *can* be just a depot-project setting.

**The one thing we haven't confirmed:** whether Google lets you post the sign at the depot-project level for *this particular* rule, or forces you to post it on the organization. That's the test I offered earlier.

Want me to run that test now — does the per-depot sign stick, or does Google force it org-wide?

message.content: What would be amazing is if we could have the same levy driven configuration regardless of whether there is an organization or not.  Do you see a test that would determine this?  Or can you already tell from APIs and websearch that we must treach each differently?

content.text:
I can largely tell already, and the answer points somewhere good.

**For the override approach — they must be different.** Org rules only exist when there's an organization. Look back at the two tests: the no-org project returned **empty** — no rule at all. You can't override a rule that isn't there. So a "set the override" step in the levy would have to be *conditional*: do it under an org, skip it without one. That's a branch, not identical config.

**But there's a path that *is* identical in both worlds: keyless.** If the roles never create a key, there's no rule to fight — under an org or not. The levy would do the exact same thing either way, because "create no key" is uniform. That's the keyless option from the memo (₣BS).

So, plainly:
- **Override path** → can't be uniform (org-only surface).
- **Keyless path** → naturally uniform.

The thing you said would be amazing is actually an argument *for* keyless over override.

If you want certainty on the first half, the small test is: try the override on the **no-org** project and watch Google refuse it (no org to hang it on). Want me to run that one test to confirm — or is this enough to settle it?

message.content: Keyless is a huge change and I'm really not prepared for it unless we are forced.  Would you try the override on no-org now?

aiTitle: Find GCP total project quota limit via gcloud

content.text:
Refused — **403, "the caller does not have permission."**

In plain terms: on a no-org project you **can't** set this override — and you can't even *get* permission to, because the permission to change org rules comes *from an organization*, and there isn't one here.

One honesty note: the error says "no permission" rather than literally "no org." But since that permission only exists when an org exists, the practical conclusion is the same:

**The override is an org-only capability. A no-org project has nothing to override and no way to authorize one.**

So your "amazing" uniform levy can't be built on the override — the two worlds genuinely differ here. The only thing that *is* uniform is keyless, which you're rightly not ready for.

That settles the test you asked for. Want to stop here and let it sit?

message.content: Can we easily query programatically to determine if there is an organization?  That would still be simple I think, if levy just handled it with detection.

content.text:
Yes — detection is genuinely trivial, and it's the cleanest idea yet. It's **one field** on the project: `parent`.

You already saw it in the comparison:
- scaleinvariant → `parent: {"type":"organization", ...}`
- gmail → `parent: null`

So the levy, right after it creates the depot project, just reads that field:
- `parent` is an organization → run the override step.
- `parent` is null → skip it.

One levy, one `if`. That fits your philosophy well.

**One thing to keep in mind so it doesn't surprise you later:** detection decides *whether* to override, but the override still needs a permission (org-policy admin) that an org admin grants to the payor **once**, by hand — that part can't be auto-detected away. So the shape is:
- Levy: auto-detect + auto-override *(automatable — goes in levy)*
- Establish: a one-time "grant the payor this permission" step, **only** for org payors *(manual — goes in handbook)*

That's still simple, and it keeps the no-org path completely untouched.

Want me to show the one-field detection running live on your depot, or stop here?

message.content: Well if we need to configure an org payor this way, then levy ought to confirm payor has the permission _before_ creation.  Is this reasonable and automatable?

content.text:
Yes on both counts — and it's the right instinct.

**Reasonable?** Very. It matches the levy's existing "check before you build" habit (it already verifies IAM propagation, API readiness, etc. before making resources), and it avoids exactly the half-built depot we had to clean up earlier today. Fail *before* creation, not midway.

**Automatable?** Yes — one read-only call. Google has a "**does this identity have permission X?**" query (`testIamPermissions`). The levy asks: "does the payor hold the set-org-policy permission?" → yes, proceed; no, stop with a clear message pointing at the one-time grant.

**One detail that makes it clean:** the check runs against the **organization** (which the levy finds from the payor's `parent`), not the depot project — because the depot doesn't exist yet at preflight time, and that permission lives at the org and flows down anyway.

So the org branch becomes: *detect org → confirm payor can set policy → create → override.* If the confirm fails, nothing got built.

Want me to run that exact permission check live on your payor now? It's read-only, and it'll tell us whether your payor *already* has the permission (likely not, since we never granted it) — which is useful to know regardless.

lastPrompt: Well if we need to configure an org payor this way, then levy ought to confirm payor has the permission _before_ creation.  Is this reasonable and automatable?

204	.. Grant role: `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`

The `tokenCreator` role enables build steps running as {rbtr_mason} (via the build JSON's `serviceAccount` field) to generate fresh access tokens for GCP API calls — required for GAR authentication (docker login, crane push). This is distinct from `serviceAccountUser` (actAs), which the {rbtr_director} holds on {rbtr_mason} for submitting builds.
206	.. {rbbc_require}
207	IAM binding successful
208	
209	//axhos_step
210	. *Submit Per-Pool Probe Builds*:
211	.. For each worker pool variant in [`tether`, `airgap`]:
212	... {rbbc_submit}
213	link:https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.builds/create[builds.create] with:
214	.... `serviceAccount`: `projects/«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»/serviceAccounts/«MASON_EMAIL»`
215	.... `options.pool.name`: full pool resource path for the variant
216	.... `options.logging`: `CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY`
217	.... `steps[0]`: posture-check assertion (builder: `gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk:alpine`)
218	..... tether asserts `https://example.com` reachable AND `https://storage.googleapis.com` reachable (curl without `-f`; any HTTP response counts as reached)
219	..... airgap asserts `https://example.com` connection blocked AND `https://storage.googleapis.com` reachable
220	.... `steps[1]`: GAR write probe (builder: `gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker`) — `FROM scratch` image with a single marker file, pushed to `«REGION»-docker.pkg.dev/«DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»/«GAR_REPOSITORY»/rbi_df/probe-«VARIANT»:probe`
221	... {rbbc_require}
222	submission returns HTTP 200/201 (build accepted). All other codes are fatal.
223	... {rbbc_await}
224	build terminal state via `builds.get` polled every `ZRBGP_BUILD_POLL_INTERVAL` seconds, with a `ZRBGP_BUILD_POLL_CEILING` ceiling (10 minutes total). SUCCESS is the expected terminal state on correct posture; FAILURE means the in-build assertion tripped (egress-posture mismatch) and operators investigate via the console link. Any other terminal state (CANCELLED / TIMEOUT / EXPIRED / INTERNAL_ERROR) indicates Cloud Build infrastructure trouble and is similarly diagnostic, not silently accepted.
225	... {rbbc_show}
226	per-pool probe block carrying the pool ID, GAR artifact reference, build ID + Cloud Build console link, and terminal status.
227	.. NOTE: Builder images are Google-hosted only (`gcr.io/...`). The airgap pool cannot reach `docker.io`; any non-Google builder would hang the build.
228	.. NOTE: Submission itself populates the per-pool concurrent-build quota row in Google's Cloud Build console UI — what the preflight diagnostic calls "after some build activity." A SUCCESS terminal probe confirms three things end-to-end: the pool can dispatch builds, the worker exhibits the egress posture stamped into its network config, and the worker can authenticate to GAR and push to `rbi_df`. Operators bump per-pool quota afterward via the now-populated console row to enable subsequent parallel gauntlet activity.
229	.. NOTE: Submission awaits terminal state inline. The two iterations of the loop run serially because the await blocks the loop body before the next submission; this is load-bearing. An earlier non-awaiting variant caused the late-clearing-probe-collision wedge — the airgap probe queued for ~19 minutes behind subsequent gauntlet dispatches, and when it finally cleared, the next tether conjure (e.g., `ordain_conjure_jupyter`) wedged in `QUEUED` until `queueTtl=3600s` expired the build. See `Memos/memo-20260517-cloudbuild-default-quota-wedge/`. The serialized await also makes the airgap probe's slot acquisition fast in the no-contention case, since no other gauntlet builds are competing during the levy window.
230	.. NOTE: In-build posture-assertion failure (e.g., airgap pool reaching the public internet, tether pool unable to resolve `storage.googleapis.com`) surfaces in the build's terminal status as FAILURE — a hard infra-drift signal. The posture-check script uses `curl` without `-f` so that HTTP 4xx/5xx responses from a reached server still count as "reached"; only honest network failures (DNS, connection refused, timeout) trip the assertion.
231	.. NOTE: The `rbi_df` namespace is a flat sibling of `rbi_hm` / `rbi_rq` / `rbi_es` under the GAR repository. {rbtgo_image_audit} / {rbtgo_image_rekon} enumerators ignore it by design — probe artifacts are operational, not part of the hallmark / reliquary / enshrine image catalogue. Filenames under `rbi_df` form a flat sea (no subdirs); each filename names one depot-scoped OCI artifact.

254	.. NOTE: The tripwire freezes the depot-time-immutable

Every cloud-submitting command thereafter pulls and exact-byte-diffs the inscribed copy against the local {rbrd_regime} assignment file before submitting, so a post-levy hand-edit fails loud rather than silently diverging from the depot's actual worker-pool quotas, region, and identity. Implemented host-side (Cloud Build is not involved) by `rbrd_inscribe` in `Tools/rbk/rbndb_base.sh`. The tripwire push runs after the depot-tracking refresh; a failure here leaves the depot live but un-inscribed, recoverable via `tt/rbw-rdi.sh` once the cause is resolved.

content.text:
Permission check result: the payor holds **none** of them (`{}`). So today, even on the org side, the payor *cannot* set the override — which means a preflight would correctly **stop the levy before building anything**. The gate works.

## The spec: `RBSDE-depot_levy.adoc`

It's a clean ordered-step spec, and **yes, there's a simple way.** Each step uses reusable building blocks (`{rbbc_call}`, `{rbbc_require}`, `{rbbc_fatal}`), and it already has "verify before you build" steps (e.g., *Verify IAM Propagation*). A preflight slots right in after step 1, *Authenticate as Payor*:

> **Confirm org-policy authority** *(org payors only)* — read the project's parent; if it's an organization, ask `testIamPermissions` for the set-policy permission; require it, else fail with "grant the payor org-policy admin first."

That's idiomatic to the spec — no new machinery, just one more guard step in the existing style. The override *action* itself would be a separate late step (after the project exists), but the preflight you asked about is the easy, clean part.

**One catch worth knowing** (and it's the same thing that bit us earlier): the spec detects org-ness from the regime field `RBRP_PARENT_TYPE` (line 47). But that field was **stale** — it said `none` while the org silently auto-parented the project. So the preflight must read the project's **live parent**, not trust the config field. Otherwise it'd wrongly skip the check.

I haven't edited anything — this is stabled ₣BS work and the override-vs-keyless choice is still open. Want me to draft the preflight step text (not commit) so you can see it concretely, or stop here?

content.text:
You're right, and it's worse than stale — it's effectively **dead**. Here's all that exists:

- **Defined** in `rbrp.env` (`=none`).
- **"Validated"** only as a generic string — `buv_string_enroll ... 1 64 "Parent resource type"`. That just checks "is it a 1–64 character string." No enum, no check that `none` is a real sentinel, no comparison to reality.
- **Used: nowhere.** Nothing reads it. The actual project create is *parentless* (`rbgp_Payor.sh:1041`, "OAuth users create projects without parent") — and then GCP silently auto-parents under the org.

So: not enforced meaningfully, not used at all. The spec (`RBSDE` line 47, "parent: …if parent_type != none") describes behavior the **code doesn't implement** — a spec/code divergence.

**Two consequences:**
1. For the preflight we discussed — **don't trust this field.** Detect org from the project's *live* parent (the query we ran), never from `RBRP_PARENT_TYPE`.
2. It's a small cleanup item for ₣BS: either wire it up properly (real enum, actually set the parent) or delete it and fix the spec. Right now it's a config knob that does nothing and misleads.

That's the confirmation. Stop here?

message.content: Keyless is _not_ for this heat, I have decided.  But didn't we also determine to 1) add steps to payor establish to grant some permissions IFF organization (needs handbook and RBS*.adoc upgrade) then 2) check before running 'levy' that payor has appropriate permissions and finally 3) if there is org during levy then do the permissions grant?

content.text:
Yes — those three are exactly what we determined, and deciding **keyless out / override in** is what unlocks them. With the fork resolved, ₣BS can move from shape to paces. Restating them precisely so the dockets are clean:

1. **Payor establish (IFF org)** — grant the payor the authority to set org policy (org-policy admin). Manual, one-time, org-admin action. → handbook `RBHPE` + `RBSPE` spec.
2. **Levy preflight (IFF org)** — before creating anything, verify the payor *holds* that authority; fail fast if not. → `RBSDE` spec.
3. **Levy action (IFF org)** — after creating the depot project, set the project-scoped override that re-enables key creation on it. → `RBSDE` spec.

(Small vocabulary note so paces stay precise: #1 is an *IAM role grant to the payor*; #3 is an *org-policy override on the depot project*. Different mechanisms — worth keeping distinct in the dockets.)

**One thing we must add before building on these:** we never actually confirmed that a project-scoped override of this *managed* constraint **takes effect**. We only proved the no-org project refuses it and the payor currently lacks permission. The research flagged that managed constraints can resist project-level override and force you org-wide. If that's true here, #2 and #3 change shape. So the safe first pace is **verify the override works at project scope** — the linchpin the other two rest on.

Proposed ₣BS pace set, in order:
- **Verify project-scoped override actually re-enables key creation** *(linchpin — do first)*
- **Delete dead `RBRP_PARENT_TYPE`/`ID` + fix RBSDE divergence** *(independent, can land anytime)*
- **Org-detection from live project parent** *(helper #2 and #3 both need)*
- **Establish: grant payor org-policy authority** *(#1)*
- **Levy preflight: confirm authority** *(#2)*
- **Levy: project-scoped override** *(#3)*

Want me to slate these into ₣BS now? I'll keep each docket tight, mark the IFF-org ones, and order them so the linchpin verification gates the rest.

We confirmed the payor has none of those permissions.

So yes, we can verify on the current depot (`canest3bhm100001`) — but I want your explicit go-ahead, because the verification is a chain of **real security mutations on your org**, not a read-only probe:

1. **Grant** `roles/orgpolicy.policyAdmin` to `bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org` (self-grant — a powerful role).
2. **Determine** which constraint is actually in force — legacy `iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` vs the managed `iam.managed.*` (this is the double-lock question, and it decides v1-vs-v2 override API).
3. **Set the project-scoped override** on `canest3bhm100001` — i.e., punch a hole in the org's key-creation baseline for that one project.
4. **Attempt to create an SA key** there → success = project-scoped override works (linchpin confirmed); still-blocked = it forces org-level (linchpin fails, the plan changes).

**All four are reversible**, and I'd clean up after: revoke the role, delete the override (restoring inherited enforcement), delete the test key. Net residue: none.

content.text:
**Stage 1 done:** policy-admin granted; test SAs identified (`mason-…` and a leftover `governor-…` from the earlier failed mantle); legacy constraint reads **enforced**. Note: the **managed-constraint (v2) query failed** — same OAuth-consumer quirk (Org Policy API not enabled on the payor project `727189059962`). So I'll take the pragmatic route: set the **v1 legacy project override** and let the key-creation test be the real verdict.

**Stage 2: set the project-scoped override, confirm it flips, then actually try to create a key.**

input.command:
cd /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster 2>/dev/null
STOK=$(cat /tmp/scinv_token)
PROJ=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
SA=mason-canest3bhm100001@cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001.iam.gserviceaccount.com

echo "=== [A] Set project-scoped override (enforced:false) ==="
curl -s -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $STOK" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/projects/${PROJ}:setOrgPolicy" \
  -d '{"policy":{"constraint":"constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation","booleanPolicy":{"enforced":false}}}' \
  | jq -c '{constraint, booleanPolicy, updateTime} // .error.message'

echo "=== [B] Confirm effective policy now reads not-enforced ==="
curl -s -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $STOK" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  "https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com/v1/projects/${PROJ}:getEffectiveOrgPolicy" \
  -d '{"constraint":"constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation"}' | jq -c '.booleanPolicy'

echo "=== [C] Attempt SA key creation (retry loop for propagation) ==="
KEYNAME=""
for i in $(seq 1 12); do

content.text:
Good caution — and the honest answer is that the risk here **isn't size**; the code is probably small. The risk is in two other places, and they're the ones that have served us well to respect all session.

## The real risks (neither is LOC)

**1. Domain mismatch — it dilutes the heat's identity.** ₣BS is *modern-organization-features* — secure-by-default org behavior. But the allocator collision is about **global projectId reservation churn**, which is **identity-orthogonal**: it bit us cross-identity (gmail vs scaleinvariant), and would bite an org-less setup just as readily. It has nothing to do with organizations. Folding it in makes ₣BS a grab-bag, and we've worked hard to keep heats coherent. That's exactly why you spun ₣BR off in the first place.

**2. The fix isn't designed — unlike the org paces.** The five we just slated rest on a *verified* mechanism. The allocator fix does not. We still don't know:
- **Where it lives** — you said "bash + RBS0," implying the *production levy* (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`). But the thing that actually collided was the *test fixture's* Rust picker (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`), which the levy doesn't even use. That ambiguity is itself the tell that it's undesigned.
- **What it does** — probe-and-skip candidate IDs? just a clearer 409? We never decided.

Doing it now means designing on the fly — the over-prescription trap you've steered around repeatedly today.

**3. Low urgency makes the trade bad.** We already mitigated it (the canest3 bump), and we *proved* same-identity self-heals — so the residual collision only recurs on a **payor-identity switch**, which is rare. Low urgency + undesigned + out-of-domain is the worst profile to "sneak in."

## My recommendation

**Keep it out of ₣BS.** Not because it's big, but because it's a *different problem*, *not yet designed*, and *not urgent*. Sneaking it in trades a coherent, verified heat for a muddier one — poor value.

The cautious move: leave it in **₣BR**, and either give ₣BR a short paddock now so the idea is durably captured (it currently lives only in this chat), or park it as-is. I'd lean toward the quick paddock — that's the small, safe thing worth doing; the implementation is what should wait.

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BR

## Shape

This heat hardens RBK against GCP behaviors that surface only under real-world churn. Current content: one problem — **depot projectId reservation collisions**.

GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after a project is deleted (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker allocator picks "next free" by walking a same-identity displayName search (`projects:search`, via depot_list). That search is identity-scoped: it returns the caller's own projects including their DELETE_REQUESTED ones — so **same-identity churn self-heals**, the allocator increments past its own pending-delete depots.

The blindness is **cross-identity**: a projectId reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project is invisible to the caller's search. The allocator then derives a moniker whose global ID is already held elsewhere, and project creation fails with 409. This bit the project when the payor switched from the gmail (no-org) identity to the scaleinvariant identity: gmail's deleted `canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to the scaleinvariant allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor.

## Scope and urgency

Low urgency. Same-identity churn already self-heals; the residual collision recurs **only on a payor-identity switch** — rare. The session that found it worked around it with a family-stem bump. This is robustness, not a blocker.

## Undecided (design, not yet settled)

- **Where the fix lives.** The collider was the *test fixture* moniker picker (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, Rust); the production levy (`rbgp_depot_levy`) does not auto-pick — it uses the configured `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` and 409s on collision. So "fixture-only Rust fix" vs "production levy change (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`/`RBSDN`)" is an open choice — different surfaces, different blast radius.
- **What the fix does.** A candidate-ID availability probe (GET → 404 free / 403 owned-elsewhere / 200 own; increment past taken) tests the real global constraint instead of the same-identity proxy — versus a lighter "clearer 409 + operator guidance" approach.

## Constraints

# jjezs_paddock ₣BR

## Shape

This heat hardens RBK against GCP behaviors that surface only under real-world churn. Current content: one problem — **depot projectId reservation collisions**.

GCP project IDs are globally unique and stay reserved ~30 days after a project is deleted (DELETE_REQUESTED). The depot-moniker allocator picks "next free" by walking a same-identity displayName search (`projects:search`, via depot_list). That search is identity-scoped: it returns the caller's own projects including their DELETE_REQUESTED ones — so **same-identity churn self-heals**, the allocator increments past its own pending-delete depots.

The blindness is **cross-identity**: a projectId reserved by a *different* identity's deleted project is invisible to the caller's search. The allocator then derives a moniker whose global ID is already held elsewhere, and project creation fails with 409. This bit the project when the payor switched from the gmail (no-org) identity to the scaleinvariant identity: gmail's deleted `canest2bhm100000` held the global ID, invisible to the scaleinvariant allocator, which kept re-deriving the family floor.

## Scope and urgency

Low urgency. Same-identity churn already self-heals; the residual collision recurs **only on a payor-identity switch** — rare. The session that found it worked around it with a family-stem bump. This is robustness, not a blocker.

## Undecided (design, not yet settled)

- **Where the fix lives.** The collider was the *test fixture* moniker picker (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs`, Rust); the production levy (`rbgp_depot_levy`) does not auto-pick — it uses the configured `RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER` and 409s on collision. So "fixture-only Rust fix" vs "production levy change (`rbgp_Payor.sh` + `RBSDE`/`RBSDN`)" is an open choice — different surfaces, different blast radius.
- **What the fix does.** A candidate-ID availability probe (GET → 404 free / 403 owned-elsewhere / 200 own; increment past taken) tests the real global constraint instead of the same-identity proxy — versus a lighter "clearer 409 + operator guidance" approach.

## Constraints

- Any bash work here reads **BCG** (Bash Console Guide) first. `rbgp_Payor.sh` is a complex BCG-compliant module; do not write bash against it without the guide.

## What done looks like

A decision on fix surface (fixture vs production) and fix shape (probe vs guidance), then the implementation plus matching spec update. Until then, paces stay uncut.
